content:
===center
![ezuri](https://i.imgur.com/9MUOZQk.jpg)

*All Will Be Compleat*

===endcenter


===accordion
===panel:Introduction

## Introduction

[[Ezuri, Renegade Leader]] was once the hope of the Elf faction on Mirrodin.  But as the Praetor grip on the plane strengthened, so too did the influence of the Progress Engine.

Ezuri could evade them no longer, and was captured.  [[Jin-Gitaxias]] experimented on him.  Everything on New Phyrexia must reach its highest potential, by any means necessary.  *Augmented, perfected, compleat.*

[[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] is the engine of New Phyrexia's unnatural evolution.  With his mutagenic abilities, every living being on the plane will be compleat.

This deck focuses on Ezuri's ability to generate Experience Counters whenever we play a creature with power 2 or less, so nearly our entire creature line-up features 2-or-less power critters.  That restriction removes a lot of the generic Simic "goodstuff" and encourages us to use a lot of fun creatures not usually found in a lot of Simic EDH decks.  Ezuri uses his Experience counters to buff one of our creatures every combat and this deck is designed to take advantage of that in every way possible.



This Primer features the following:

- **The Gameplan** - for those who just want the general, bare-minimum game plan

- **Pros, Cons & Power Level** - explaining the strengths and weaknesses of this deck

- **Packages** - cards grouped together so you can have a better idea of how many sources we have of ramp, card draw, removal, etc.

- **Individual Card Choices** - every single card discussed individually to justify its inclusion in the deck and specific interactions

- **Combos** - step-by-step combos explained so you can demonstrate them to opponents in a live game

- **No-Combo Kit** - certain cards may rub some play groups the wrong way, this section points them out so you can remove them and substitute different cards to satisfy a lower-power playgroup

- **Upgrades** - if you want to make this deck even more over-the-top or damn near cEDH level

- **Budget Options** - a significantly cheaper version of this deck

- **Counter Stacking Effects Explained** - an in-depth explanation of how effects like [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]] and [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]] work alongside each other

- **Change Log** - any edits made to the deck itself and why those changes occurred, witness the deck evolve over time exactly how the Progress Engine intended

===endpanel
===panel:The Gameplan

## The Gameplan

===accordion
===panel:Early Game

Get [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] on the board if and only if you have a 2-or-less-power creature ready to play afterward.  We want to capitalize on his ability and get an Experience Counter right away (ideally on the same turn, however next turn is fine).  There's no need to put him out there if he's not building experience.  Note that this does NOT mean hold back your mana dorks like [[Llanowar Elves]].  Cast them before Ezuri if you have them so you can turbo him out even quicker!  

However, if you have a choice of [[Farseek]] vs a mana dork, for example, prioritize the spell-based ramp that'll get lands into play.  Putting all of your eggs in the creature basket early on is going to be feel really bad if an opponent wipes the board.  Building a solid mana foundation will help you recover after a setback.

===endpanel
===panel:Mid Game

Speaking of setbacks, try not to vomit your whole hand onto the field even if you have the mana to do so.  This deck can apply a lot of pressure with just a few beefed up creatures, so play smart and try not to overextend.  I'm not saying keep a whole hand of 7 at all times, but hold some resources in case of emergencies.

Protect Ezuri while playing weak creatures to gain Experience counters.  Prioritize creatures like [[Siren Stormtamer]] and [[Mausoleum Wanderer]] if you have them, as they help protect Ezuri and deter early-game removal (opponents won't want to waste resources on Ezuri if they could rather hit something else or another player).

Put +1/+1 counters on evasive (unblockable/flying) creatures and hit hard at your opponents' life totals.  Try to spread the counters each turn; having several semi-strong creatures makes your board more resilient than one ultra-powerful creature.  After you've built a board of, say, three or four strong creatures you can start stacking counters on the most dangerous one to really apply pressure on opponents.

===endpanel
===panel:Late Game

If the opportunity presents itself, look to use [[Ouroboroid]], [[Cultivator of Blades]] or [[Wild Beastmaster]] as finishers.  [[Herald of Secret Streams]] and/or [[Skatewing Spy]] can help us push damage past blockers.

[[Giggling Skitterspike]] is another win-con, as it essentially deals its power worth of combat damage to all opponents any time it attacks, blocks or becomes targeted by a spell.  And being indestructible makes it a very difficult-to-answer ticking time bomb for our opponents.

If the game hasn't ended, find a way to tutor [[Sage of Hours]] and start taking infinite turns to close out the game (this combo is explained in detail in the **Combos** section of this primer).  Some reliable tutors that can find it are [[Finale of Devastation]], [[Archdruid's Charm]] and [[Muddle the Mixture]].

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center

![bird](https://i.imgur.com/OvWfqlv.jpg)

*Even the smallest of subjects can be a catalyst for perfection*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Pros, Cons & Power Level

## Pros, Cons & Power Level

✅ **Pros**

- Very easy to pilot, most of your turns will be very short and sweet.

- This deck avoids a lot of the generic Simic goodstuff and common finishers like [[Craterhoof Behemoth|MM3]] in favor of more niche, low-power creatures and evasion to deal big damage.

- It's very customizable.  Ezuri only cares about 2 or less power.  Very few of the creatures in this particular list would be considered "must-have", you can play what you like for the most part and still have a good time.

❌ **Cons**

- Being creature focused, board wipes will hurt.  We have some counterspells to answer these, but the point stands that getting wiped feels bad.

- With a less-than-ideal opening hand, this deck may take a while to build up steam.  Ezuri also needs to be on the field to generate experience counters.

☢️ **Power Level**

- This deck is high-power casual.

- You can win fairly quickly if you get enough experience and find [[Sage of Hours]].  For that reason I try not go for the combo and save it as a last resort.  Combat damage is the main win-con.

===endpanel
===panel:Packages

## Packages

===accordion
===panel:Ramp (20)

- [[Birds of Paradise]], [[Elvish Mystic]], [[Fyndhorn Elves]], [[Llanowar Elves]], [[Gyre Sage]], [[Incubation Druid]], [[Heronblade Elite]], [[Marwyn, the Nurturer]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]], [[Sol Ring]], [[Ornithopter of Paradise]], [[Thought Vessel]] and [[Maraleaf Pixie]] all generate mana on their own.


- [[Kodama of the West Tree]], [[Sakura-Tribe Elder]], [[Archdruid's Charm]], [[Farseek]], [[Rampant Growth]], [[Three Visits]] and [[Nature's Lore]] all get lands out of our deck and onto the battlefield.

===endpanel
===panel:Card Draw & Tutors (14)

- [[Terrasymbiosis]] draws us cards whenever we put +1/+1 counters on a creature (but it only triggers once per turn so we have to make sure we get the most out of it).

- [[Ray Fillet, Wave Warrior]], [[Toski, Bearer of Secrets]], [[Ohran Frostfang]] and [[Bred for the Hunt]] draw us cards whenever our creatures deal combat damage.


- [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] draws us cards whenever it deals combat damage to a player.


- [[Beast Whisperer]] draw us a card whenever we cast a creature.


- [[Faerie Mastermind]] can draw us a card with its activated ability and/or whenever an opponent draws their second card each turn.


- [[Pollywog Prodigy]] draws us a card whenever an opponents casts any non-creature spell with CMC less than its power.


- [[Fathom Mage]] draws us a card for each +1/+1 counter placed on it.


- [[Inspiring Call]] is a one-time card draw effect.


- [[Waterlogged Grove]] can be sacrificed to draw a card.


- [[Archdruid's Charm]] can tutor for any creature, useful for finding a finisher or combo piece.


- [[Muddle the Mixture]] can transmute and tutor for any card with CMC 2.

===endpanel
===panel:Removal & Interaction (17)

- [[Boseiju, Who Endures]] can destroy an artifact, enchantment or non-basic land with its Channel ability.

- [[Otawara, Soaring City]] can bounce nearly any permanent with its Channel ability.

- [[Rapid Hybridization]] and [[Pongify]] deal with creatures.

- [[Bridgeworks Battle]] can force fights between creatures.

- [[Beast Within]] deals with any permanent.

- [[Cyclonic Rift]] returns any non-land permanent to hand (or all enemy permanents if we overload it).

- [[Archdruid's Charm]] can get rid of an artifact, enchantment or creature if we have one to fight with.

- [[Hypnotic Siren]] doesn't remove a creature but it does let us take control of it with its Bestow mode, arguably more effective than removal.

- [[Glen Elendra Archmage]], [[Siren Stormtamer]] and [[Mausoleum Wanderer]] can all be sacrificed to counter spells at instant speed.

- [[Glen Elendra Guardian]], [[An Offer You Can't Refuse]], [[Swan Song]], [[Muddle the Mixture]] and [[Fierce Guardianship]] can all counter spells on the stack.

===endpanel
===panel:Yo Dawg, I Heard You Like Counters (10)

- [[Benevolent Hydra]], [[Innkeeper's Talent]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]] and [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]] all increase the number of counters generated by any effect that generates (or moves) counters.


- [[Thrummingbird]] can increase existing counters on permanents and players.


- [[Ouroboroid]], [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]], [[Grove's Bounty]], [[Archdruid's Charm]] and [[Innkeeper's Talent]] all have abilities that give other creatures +1/+1 counters.


===endpanel
===panel:Fightin' Dirty (18)

- [[Blighted Agent]] and [[Invisible Stalker]] are unblockable.  [[Elusive Otter]] can basically become unblockable when we make it big enough.


- [[Birds of Paradise]], [[Ornithopter of Paradise]], [[Hypnotic Siren]], [[Mausoleum Wanderer]], [[Siren Stormtamer]], [[Thrummingbird]], [[Glen Elendra Archmage]], [[Glen Elendra Guardian]] and [[Inkmoth Nexus]] all have Flying so they are difficult to block and can swing for big damage if loaded up with +1/+1 counters.


- [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] can freely attack anybody who controls an Island.


- [[Champion of Lambholt]] and [[Herald of Secret Streams]] can make creatures unblockable.


- [[Kodama of the West Tree]], [[Sphere Grid]] and [[Skatewing Spy]] can give our creatures keywords to give them an edge in combat or evade it entirely.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center

![besieged](https://i.imgur.com/EETx6RT.jpg)

*Every piece, organic and metallic, serves only to fuel the Progress Engine*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Individual Cards Explained

## Individual Cards Explained

A lot of the cards in this deck are pretty self-explanatory.  *Reading the card explains the card.*  However, some do have some niche applications and synergies that aren't immediately apparent.  Take a glance through this section if any card in the list looks peculiar to you, otherwise feel free to collapse this panel and skip it.  You can collapse it by clicking/tapping the purple heading just above.

I also prefer to discuss my cards in order of ascending CMC to better visualize my curve.  If you're viewing this deck, I encourage you to click the "**Sort**" dropdown and select "**Mana Value**".

You can click/tap the heading of each category below to collapse it and make scrolling a little faster.

===accordion
===panel:Commander

[[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] isn't too complex of a commander but let's disect it just so we're on the same page.

**"Whenever a creature with power 2 less enters the battlefield under your control, you get an Experience Counter."**

The word "whenever" means this ability triggers for EACH creature that meets the requirement, regardless of whether they ETB'd individually or together.  Let's say we have a [[Chasm Skulker]] with a bunch of +1/+1 counters and it dies.  We then make a bunch of 1/1 Squid tokens.  Each of those tokens with trigger Ezuri, meaning we'll get Exp counters equal to the number of tokens that entered.

Keep in mind that state-based effects increasing our creatures' power will interfere with Ezuri's ability.  For example, if we have a [[Murkfiend Liege]] on the field and play an [[Ice-Fang Coatl]], it will enter the field as a 3/3 and therefore will NOT trigger Ezuri's ability.  This is why we're not running any anthem effects.

Ezuri's last ability is the payoff for generating so many experience counters.  At the beginning of combat, we get to place +1/+1 counters on any other creature equal to the number of experience counters we have.  With this effect, we can make one of our weak creatures absolutely huge.  This deck is packed full of ways to make these creatures evasive, allowing them to slip past any blockers and deal massive damage to our opponents.

Keep in mind that experience counters are placed on players, not Ezuri, so if he is removed from the field, we retain any Exp counters we have earned and can just cast Ezuri later to start gaining them again on top of the ones we already have.

===endpanel
===panel:Creatures

**NOTE:** Nearly every creature in the deck has a base power of 2 or less, so pretty much all of them will trigger [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]]'s effect.  The creature package was carefully curated to work well with our commander but also have strong and synergistic effects of their own.


[[Elvish Mystic]], [[Fyndhorn Elves]] and [[Llanowar Elves]] are all reliable, cheap, early-game mana dorks.


[[Birds of Paradise]] is another cheap mana dork but taps for either of our colors.


[[Hypnotic Siren]] at its worst is just a 1/1 with Flying for just the cost of [[symbol:u]], so it's an easy Exp counter and an evasive creature that can be loaded up with +1/+1 counters for big damage.  If we draw it in the early game, we can use it as a beater.  However, if we find it in the late game, we can cast it for its Bestow cost [[symbol:5]][[symbol:u]][[symbol:u]] and use it to take control of an enemy creature.  This is a great way to shut down an enemy commander without making it change zones so its owner cannot move it back to their command zone.  [[Hypnotic Siren]] is just a versatile card in this deck.


[[Mausoleum Wanderer]] is cheap to cast, triggers Ezuri and has Flying which gives it decent evasion.  Furthermore, it can sac itself to counter an instant or sorcery unless the caster pays [[symbol:x]], with X being the Wanderer's power.  If we put a bunch of +1/+1 counters on it, X is going to be a fairly big number so it'll either counter the spell or force our opponent to pay so much mana to make sure it resolves that they'll be unlikely to have mana for further plays.


[[Siren Stormtamer]] is fantastic in this deck because not only does it trigger Ezuri upon ETB, but it has Flying which makes it a prime candidate to buff with +1/+1 counters.  And finally, it gives us the option to sacrifice it to counter a spell or ability that targets one of our creatures (or us directly).


[[Elusive Otter]] has great utility in this deck.  If we draw it in the early game, we can play it as a 1-cost creature and load it up with +1/+1 counters which will quickly make it unblockable thanks to its ability.  If, however, we draw it in the late game, we can sink a ton of mana into its [[Grove's Bounty]] adventure spell and give a our other creatures +1/+1 counters, which synergizes nicely if we have things like [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]] in play.  Then, we can spend just [[symbol:u]] more to get the [[Elusive Otter]] anyway!


[[Ornithopter of Paradise]] and [[Maraleaf Pixie]] serve double duty as mana dorks but also as Flying attackers if loaded up with +1/+1 counters.


[[Gyre Sage]] can tap for green mana equal to the number of +1/+1 counters on it.  With [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]], we can put all of our experience on [[Gyre Sage]] and tap it for massive amounts of mana.


[[Incubation Druid]] taps for 1 mana of either Blue or Green (in our deck) but if it has at least 1 +1/+1 counter on it, it produces 3 mana instead.  The ceiling is much lower than [[Gyre Sage]] but it does at least have a self sufficient way of helping itself out with the Adapt ability.


[[Sakura-Tribe Elder]] can sacrifice itself at instant speed to tutor a basic land onto our field.  It's a great blocker because we can put it in front of an attacking creature and sac it before damage calculation, thus fizzling the attack and getting a land out of our deck.


[[Invisible Stalker]] is very difficult for opponents to deal with due to Hexproof.  The best part is that it's unblockable, so we can stack a ton of +1/+1 counters on it at the start of combat with Ezuri's effect and swing for massive, unblockable combat damage.


[[Blighted Agent]] is another unblockable threat but this one has Infect, meaning it only needs to deal 10 points of damage to a single player to take them out of the game.  Not every playgroup will be ok with it, however, so be sure to let them know you are playing it and replace it with another creature before the game starts if they are not ok with Infect.


[[Thrummingbird]] lets us Proliferate whenever it deals damage to an opponent, which is pretty much laid out right on the bird's effect text.  We choose any permanents with counters on them and give them one more of every kind of counter they have.  This can include our creatures with +1/+1 counters, our Planeswalkers with Loyalty counters, us with Experience counters and any opponents with Poison counters.  That's pretty much everything we should be proliferating whenever we get the chance.


[[Faerie Mastermind]] is fantastic card in this deck.  2 power threshold for Ezuri, check.  Built-in evasion for easy combat damage, check.  Already off to a great start, however this guy can also draw us a card whenever an opponent draws their second card each turn.  Furthermore, it has its own self-sufficient way of triggering this effect with its activated ability that draws everyone a card.  Also notice how it says "Whenever **an opponent**", so it can trigger for each opponent who draws two cards per turn.  So keep track of that, don't just pay attention to whose turn it is, be sure to get your draw if another player at the table uses something like [[Brainstorm]] on another opponent's turn.


[[Pollywog Prodigy]] is a really cool staxy creature that draws us a card whenever an opponent casts a non-creature spell with CMC less than the frog's power.  Normally we'd have to rely on its Evolve ability to grow it but in this deck we can just move to combat and dump a ton of +1/+1 counters on it thanks to Ezuri and then it essentially turns every single non-creature spell our opponents cast into a free "Draw a card" for us!


[[Sage of Hours]] lets us take an extra turn if we remove at least five +1/+1 counters from it using its effect.  If we have at least five Exp counters and [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] on board, we can essentially take infinite turns by putting counters on the Sage during each combat, not swinging and just removing those counters at instant speed to take an extra turn and repeat.


[[Benevolent Hydra]] is a bit like [[Hardened Scales]] with extra utility.  First off, we can cast it for just [[symbol:g]][[symbol:g]] which means it will enter the battlefield as a 1/1 which will trigger Ezuri.  Or we can sink as much mana into it as we want and make it enter with additional +1/+1 counters.  The real value, however, is that it increases the number of +1/+1 counters that would be placed on our creatures by any effect by one.  And the hydra itself can redistribute one of its own counters to another creature with its activated ability, so that counter will actually turn into two thanks to its own static effect.


[[Kami of Whispered Hopes]] is another amazing utility creature that increases the amount of +1/+1 counters that would be placed on our creatures.  It also has 1 base power, so it gives us that all-important experience counter.  And finally, it can tap to add mana equal to its power, so if we load this little Kami up with counters, we benefit with more mana.  Just like [[Marwyn, the Nurturer]] and [[Heronblade Elite]].


[[Champion of Lambholt]] grows stronger with each creature we play but the great thing is that it makes enemy creatures with lesser power than it unable to block our attacking creatures.  If we use [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] to buff [[Champion of Lambholt]], we can make it insanely powerful without having to wait for it to power up on its own.  Then, most of our creatures will be able to attack unimpeded.


[[Wild Beastmaster]] is one of our finishers in this deck if the [[Sage of Hours]] combo can't be assembled.  Whenever she attacks, she gives all of our other creautures +X/+X where X is her power.  So we take all of our available +1/+1 counters from [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]]'s trigger and stack them on her, turn her sideways and all of our other creatures get the same kind of buff.  This, alongside with [[Cultivator of Blades]], is one of the best finishers in the deck.


[[Shroofus Sproutsire]] is good in this deck because he has built-in Trample, making him an ideal candidate to buff up with +1/+1 counters.  However what makes him truly insane is that whenever ANY saproling deals combat damage to an opponent (not just him), we create that same number of 1/1 Saproling tokens.  More counters for Ezuri and an ever-growing board for us.


[[Ray Fillet, Wave Warrior]] is yet another evasive threat that can be loaded up with counters for big damage but more importantly his static ability to let us draw cards whenever one of our creatures with a counter connects with combat damage essentially acts like another copy of [[Bred for the Hunt]].


[[Marwyn, the Nurturer]] is mainly in here to generate massive amounts of mana with her last ability.  She can grow stronger herself every time we play an Elf but we can always shortcut by giving her a bunch of +1/+1 counters from [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]]'s ability.  We actually have quite a few elves in the 99 so growing Marwyn with her own ability is not impossible.


[[Heronblade Elite]] is incredibly similar to Marwyn except she grows whenever we play a Human.  However she has some additional benefits over Marwyn as well, notably that she can tap for mana of any color so we can produce blue with her as well as green.  Finally, she has Vigilance which makes her easy to swing with as she'll remain untapped and ready to block on the crackback.


[[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] lets us draw cards equal to the amount of combat damage it does to an enemy player.  We have plenty of ways of making things unblockable so it's not unreasonable to expect that this will be drawing us A LOT of cards once it's been buffed by [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]].  Even on its own, it has Islandwalk, so if there is a blue player at the table, be sure to take advantage of that if our hand is running low on cards.


[[Glen Elendra Guardian]] has the neat ability to counter a non-creature spell on the stack if we pay [[symbol:1]][[symbol:u]] and remove a counter from it.  We can load him up with ammunition thanks to Ezuri and then he simply an on-demand [[Negate]], making it extremely difficult for opponents to interact with us.


[[Kodama of the West Tree]] essentially gives Trample to all of our creatures that have +1/+1 counters so opponents can't just prevent damage by chump blocking.  [[Herald of Secret Streams]] helps us get around the problem, [[Skatewing Spy]] helps us get over the problem, [[Kodama of the West Tree]] lets us go through the problem.  Furthermore, whenever one of those creatures deals damage to our opponents, we get to ramp which is made incredibly easy because Kodama gives them Trample.


[[Fathom Mage]] is an insanely good card draw engine in this deck because it draws us a card for EACH +1/+1 counter that is put on it, even if multiple are placed on it at one time, it still counts each one and draws us a card for each one.


[[Herald of Secret Streams]] makes all of our creatures with +1/+1 counters unblockable.  Not much to say here, it triggers Ezuri and fascilitates our game plan by allowing our creatures to swing freely.


[[Skatewing Spy]] gives each of our creatures with a +1/+1 counter Flying, giving them evasion to help avoid blockers.  This sort of functions as a backup [[Herald of Secret Streams]].  Remember, we want to go tall and attack to get in big damage.  He also has Adapt 2, allowing us to sink mana into him late game to make him even stronger.


[[Glen Elendra Archmage]] has insanely good synergy and utility in this deck, let's break it down.  First off, it will trigger Ezuri and get us an Exp counter when we play it.  After that, we can sacrifice it to counter any non-creature spell.  Then, it comes back with Persist, meaning it will trigger Ezuri again and have a -1/-1 counter placed on it which prevents Persist from reviving it again.  However, Magic rules state that if a -1/-1 counter and a +1/+1 counter are placed on a creature, they cancel each other out and are both removed from that creature.  So if we have a [[Glen Elendra Archmage]] that has persisted, we can load it with at least one +1/+1 counter from any number of effects and it essentially deletes that -1/-1 counter, allowing us to counter another non-creature spell and [[Glen Elendra Archmage]] will be reanimated due to Persist all over again.  Fantastic synergy and repeatable [[Negate]]s.


[[Beast Whisperer]] draws us a card whenever we cast a creature, a useful way of keeping our hand full of gas since we're running so many cheap spells.


[[Toski, Bearer of Secrets]] synergizes with Ezuri, has Indestructible making it difficult to get rid of, and draws us a card whenever one of our creatures deals combat damage to somebody.  It does everything we want, excellect card for this deck especially considering how many evasive creatures we have that will be connecting with combat damage.


[[Ouroboroid]] is absolutely insane.  At the beginning of combat, we can put +1/+1 counters on EVERY CREATURE WE CONTROL equal the the number of +1/+1 counters on [[Ouroboroid]].  Guess who else triggers at the beginning of combat?  That's right, [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] can load up our [[Ouroboroid]] and then [[Ouroboroid]] can share the love with the rest of the squad.  Absolutely ridiculous effect printed on a 4-mana creature with no downsides.


[[Giggling Skitterspike]] is another extremely strong Indestructible creature.  Every time it attacks, blocks or becomes targeted by a spell, it immediately nukes all opponents for damage equal to its power.  1 base power doesn't seem so threatening but I don't think I need to remind you who our commander is and how quickly this deck can load a creature up with +1/+1 counters.


[[Cultivator of Blades]] has Fabricate 2, meaning we get to create two 1/1 Servo tokens when it enters the battlefield.  This means three Ezuri triggers, gaining us three experience counters.  The Cultivator's last ability is insanely good in this deck; when it attacks, it gives every other creature a +X/+X buff where X is equal to its power.  Normally, 1 power isn't too impressive but remember that [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] will put a ton of +1/+1 counters on any creature we want as we move to combat, so if we put them all on [[Cultivator of Blades]] and attack with it, it'll be giving that same huge buff to all of our other creatures.


[[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]] has the second half of [[Doubling Season]] stapled onto it, doubling the counters we receive along with our permanents.  Double the +1/+1 counters and double the Experience counters.  Furthermore, it effectively halves the number of counters our opponents put on permanents.  Vorinclex himself is an excellent creature to put Ezuri's counters on, because he has Trample and he'll double however many counters would be placed on him.  Let's say we have 5 experience counters when we play [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]].  We move to combat and [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] triggers.  Normally, we'd place five +1/+1 counters on a creature, but Vorinclex turns that into 10.  If we put them on him, we have a 16/16 with Trample and Haste.  That's insanely scary and this Praetor can decimate opponents out of nowhere if they don't have an answer for it.

===endpanel
===panel:Enchantments

[[Innkeeper's Talent]] starts off as a simple [[Luminarch Aspirant]]-type effect, allocating us a free +1/+1 counter at the beginning of combat.  However, we can sink mana into it to level it up to its next phase which provides our creatures a bit of protection but the real value is in its final level which turns it into a [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider]]-type effect, doubling any counters that would be put on our creatures.  A fantastic enchantment that scales into the late game.


[[Sphere Grid]] ensures that our buffed up creatures are able to push damage through blockers by granting them trample while also continuing to buff them every time they connect with combat damage.


[[Bred for the Hunt]] rewards us for playing a creature-heavy, aggressive +1/+1 counter deck.  Each time any of them deal combat damage to a player, we draw a card.  More fuel to keep the Progress Engine rolling.


===endpanel
===panel:Instants

[[Pongify]] and [[Rapid Hybridization]] are just cheap, 1-mana removal spells that can get rid of a problematic creature.


[[An Offer You Can't Refuse]] and [[Swan Song]] are simply cheap, 1-mana counterspells to help protect Ezuri while he's on the field.  The deck relies on him to really push its gameplan so cheap counterspells that deal with non-creature removal aimed at him are paramount to our gameplan.


[[Muddle the Mixture]] can be used defensively to counter an Instant or Sorcery, or we can Transmute it to find one of our valuable 2-CMC cards.  Need some evasive threat to put Ezuri counters on?  Go get [[Blighted Agent]].  Need to reanimate something?  Go get [[Finale of Devastation]].  Coast is clear and we can start taking infinite turns?  Go get [[Sage of Hours]].  Need to wipe the board?  Go get [[Cyclonic Rift]].  Lots of great options.


[[Heroic Intervention]] is just a reliable board protection spell, especially necessary in a creature-focused deck such as this.


[[Cyclonic Rift]] can serve as quick, targeted removal or as mass removal if we overload it with 7 mana.


[[Inspiring Call]] serves double duty as a reactive protection spell but also as a way to refill our hand.


[[Beast Within]] is just a reliable removal spell for any permanent on the board.  Sure the controller gets a 3/3 beast token but odds are if we're casting this, the thing we're destroying is more threatening than a vanilla 3/3.


[[Archdruid's Charm]] is an insanely versatile card.  We can use it to exile an artifact or enchantment, which is very good because it lets us deal with stuff like [[Darksteel Forge]].  We can also use it to buff one of our creatures with a +1/+1 counter (which synergizes nicely with our gameplan) and then have it fight an opponent's creature, functioning as removal.  Or we can simply use it to ramp with a land or find any creature we want, very useful for setting up a win with [[Sage of Hours]] or pushing damage in the late game with something like [[Herald of Secret Streams]].


[[Fierce Guardianship]] is a free [[Negate]] so long as we control [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]], useful for stopping a scary non-creature spell if we're tapped out and great for protecting our combo with [[Sage of Hours]] when we attempt to take our first extra turn.

===endpanel
===panel:Sorcery Spells

[[Rampant Growth]], [[Farseek]], [[Nature's Lore]] and [[Three Visits]] all ramp us by getting a land out of our deck and onto the board.


[[Bridgeworks Battle]] is a versatile little card because we can play it as an untapped land if we just need the mana.  But if we don't, we can cast it on its main side to force two creatures to fight each other, which can serve as one-sided removal if ours is strong enough to survive a fight with our opponent's.


[[Turntimber Symbiosis]] gives us something to do with any excess mana we may have.  Being able to look at the top 7 cards and cheating a creature into play is already pretty strong, but if it happens to be 3-CMC or less, it gets three +1/+1 counters which works nicely with our overall strategy.  The nice thing about this card is that if we draw it early game and need the land drop, we can simply play it as an untapped green land [[Turntimber, Serpentine Wood]], but if we draw it late game and have a ton of mana already, we can cast it for its Sorcery front side.  A nice versatile card in this deck.

[[Finale of Devastation]] is generally useful for finding a creature we need to end the game or crucially, reanimating one from a graveyard if necessary.  It's also searchable via [[Muddle the Mixture]]'s Transmute mode.

===endpanel
===panel:Lands

[[Command Tower]], [[Barkchannel Pathway]], [[Botanical Sanctum]], [[Breeding Pool]], [[Dreamroot Cascade]], [[Flooded Grove]], [[Hinterland Harbor]], [[Rejuvenating Springs]], [[Vineglimmer Snarl]], [[Waterlogged Grove]] and [[Yavimaya Coast]] can all enter untapped and produce either of our colors.


[[Prismatic Vista]] and [[Misty Rainforest]] fix our mana and thin our deck while doing it.


[[Hedge Maze]] enters tapped but can be fetched with [[Farseek]], [[Three Visits]], [[Nature's Lore]] or [[Misty Rainforest]].


[[Dryad Arbor]] can be fetched with [[Three Visits]], [[Nature's Lore]] and [[Misty Rainforest]] and since it's a 1/1 creature, it gives us an experience counter if [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] is on the field.


[[Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth]] turns all of our lands into Forests, allowing them to tap for green mana.  This is useful because we have a few cards with intensive green casting costs.


[[Boseiju, Who Endures]] has virtually no downside when compared to a regular [[Forest|ONE:266]].  We can play it untapped and it produces [[symbol:g]] if that's what we need.  However, if we find this in the late game and don't need the mana, we can hold it in hand and surprise an opponent with its Channel ability, allowing us to destroy an artifact, enchantment or non-basic land.


[[Otawara, Soaring City]] has virtually no downside when compared to a regular [[Island|ONE:263]].  We can play it untapped and it produces [[symbol:u]] if that's what we need.  However, if we find this in the late game and don't need the mana, we can hold it in hand and surprise an opponent with its Channel ability, allowing us to bounce nearly any permanent type back to its owner's hand.


[[Inkmoth Nexus]] is only in here because it can turn itself into a Flying creature with Infect.  If we have a lot of exp counters, we can use Ezuri's trigger to put the +1/+1 counters on [[Inkmoth Nexus]] (be sure to turn it into a creature before combat starts) and if our opponents have no flyers, we can swing over them for Infect damage.


10 [[Forest|ONE:266]]s and 5 [[Island|ONE:263]]s round out the basic lands in the deck.  We're playing more forests than islands because the majority of our spells require green mana to cast, especially a lot of the early game mana dorks and ramp spells.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center

![corruption](https://i.imgur.com/eKfnWJ1.jpg)

*Every being on this plane will be consumed by the inevitable tide of corruption*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Combos

## Combos

===center
![hours](https://i.imgur.com/c3ji08w.jpg)
===endcenter

**Requirements:**

- [[Sage of Hours]] and [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] on the field

- At least 5 Experience counters by the time we move to combat

**Steps:**

1. Move to combat, Ezuri triggers and we put 5 +1/+1 counters on [[Sage of Hours]].  

2. We don't have to attack with it, no need to put it at risk.

3. At any point after that, we can remove all +1/+1 counters from [[Sage of Hours]] and take an extra turn.

**Notes:**

- On that extra turn, we draw a card and have another option to advance our board state or deal more combat damage.  We move to combat, Ezuri triggers and we put 5 (or more) +1/+1 counters on [[Sage of Hours]] again.

- Then we can remove them again and take another extra turn.  See where this is going?  It's pretty straightforward.  We just take infinite turns and eventually just beat everyone down.  Just make sure [[Sage of Hours]] is never put in danger by attacking.  Eventually we will find a [[Herald of Secret Streams]] or [[Kodama of the West Tree]] to ensure that [[Sage of Hours]] can push damage through.

- This is best attempted while our opponents are tapped out or we know for sure that there is no instant-speed interaction available to them.

===endpanel
===panel:No-Combo Kit

## No-Combo Kit

Some playgroups may not appreciate you taking infinite turns.  If that's the case, be sure to remove [[Sage of Hours]] from the deck.  If that is removed, you can potentially consider removing [[Muddle the Mixture]] because it's mainly in here to Transmute for [[Sage of Hours]].  

Other playgroups might hate dying to Infect.  If so, be sure to remove [[Blighted Agent]] and [[Inkmoth Nexus]].

With those removed, make up the space with any of the cards from [this list](https://www.moxfield.com/decks/JfZwRrjiGUmaxdPKRcVO4A)

Be sure to choose replacements that are similar in function and mana cost.  For example, [[Inkmoth Nexus]] should be replaced with another land such as [[Novijen, Heart of Progress]], [[Blighted Agent]] should be replaced with an evasive creature such as [[Gudul Lurker]], etc.

===center

![adjustments](https://i.imgur.com/tW1t3y6.jpg)

*Just a few more adjustments*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Upgrades

## Upgrades

If you'd like to push this deck even further, there are a couple of changes you can make.

1. Cut some lands in favor of fast mana rocks like [[Chrome Mox]], [[Mana Crypt]], [[Jeweled Lotus]].  These will help get Ezuri on board more quickly and just overall accelerate your curve significantly.

2. Cut some of the "dead weight" or "win more" creatures in favor of some more interaction.  Examples include [[Mana Drain]] and [[Miscast]].  Creatures that fit the bill but also work with Ezuri are good here as well, examples of those would be [[Sylvan Safekeeper]] and [[Malevolent Hermit]].

3. You can also include additional combos instead of just relying on [[Sage of Hours]].  You can find some examples [here](https://edhrec.com/combos/ezuri-claw-of-progress).

A common combo line in a lot of high-level Simic decks is [[Aluren]] + any creature bounce effect ([[Shrieking Drake]], [[Dream Stalker]], [[Man-o'-War]]) + any card draw effect like [[Glimpse of Nature]], [[Beast Whisperer]], [[Soul of the Harvest]], etc.  This lets you draw your whole deck.  In our list, we can leverage this to not only draw our deck but get a ton of Exp Counters with Ezuri, cast most of our creatures then take an extra turn with [[Sage of Hours]] to swing for game afterward.

You can also look to add [[Umbral Mantle]] and/or [[Staff of Domination]] since it can generate infinite mana with things like [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]], [[Marwyn, the Nurturer]] or [[Gyre Sage]] if they're big enough.

===center
![progresstyrant](https://faithlessbrewing.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/Jin-Gitaxias-Progress-Tyrant-MTG-Art.png)

*We do not stop at perfection, we exceed it*
===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Budget Options

## Budget Options

A sample $90 budget version of this deck can be found [here](https://www.moxfield.com/decks/JfZwRrjiGUmaxdPKRcVO4A)

Lots of great options in there, cards that work well with Ezuri and don't break the bank.  Hover over the cards in this list and swap them out for cards of the same type from the budget list linked above.  You're going to want to stick to 33 or more creatures (because that's what Ezuri cares about the most) and make sure you have plenty of mana dorks among them.

You can also save a lot of money on lands by just playing the slower tapped lands rather than things like [[Breeding Pool]] and [[Misty Rainforest]].  Start there before making swaps elsewhere.

The great thing about [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]] is that he really doesn't require the most expensive cards to be good.  You can play budget creatures and still have a good time.  I encourage you to playtest the budget deck linked above and see for yourself.

All of that aside, if I wanted to make a budget dent in this particular list, here’s where I would start:

1. [[Boseiju, Who Endures]], [[Otawara, Soaring City]], [[Prismatic Vista]] and [[Fabled Passage]] can all come out.  Replace them with 2 [[Forest|ONE:266]]s and 2 [[Island|ONE:263]]s and you’ll save about $65.

2. [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider]] is good but not required.  This one could also be replaced with any budget 4, 5 or 6 drop that works well with Ezuri.  [[Defiler of Vigor]], [[Iridescent Hornbeetle]] or [[Tanazir Quandrix]] could be good replacements.

4. [[Fierce Guardianship]] is a $35 card that can be swapped for another piece of protection like [[Slip Out the Back]], [[Ripples of Potential]] or [[Negate]].

5. [[Swan Song]] is a bit pricey at $10 but in this deck, [[Stubborn Denial]] could be a good substitution.

6. [[Cyclonic Rift]] can also be replaced with any of the aforementioned board wipes, or you can run some more targeted removal like [[Tail Swipe]], [[Nature’s Claim]] or [[Return to Nature]].

7. [[Heroic Intervention]] is another $10 protection piece but [[Ripples of Potential]] is a good substitute.

===center

![glorious](https://i.imgur.com/MxUTjGT.jpg)

*All are welcome in New Phyrexia, no contribution is too small*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Counter Stacking Effects Explained

## Counter Stacking Effects Explained

I feel like this needed its own section because effects like [[Hardened Scales]] + [[Branching Evolution]] can be a little tricky for newer players.

**If you have multiple counter stacking effects on the board at the same time, you get to choose how to stack them.**

Lets start off simple with an example:

- You have [[Hardened Scales]] and [[Branching Evolution]] in play

- You cast [[Duskshell Crawler]] and choose to put the +1/+1 counter on itself.  How many counters does it get?

Well, it depends:

- If you take [[Branching Evolution]] into account first, the amount of +1/+1 counters being placed is doubled.  So 1 turns into 2.  But then you have to take [[Hardened Scales]] into account, which adds another.  So 2 turns into 3.  [[Duskshell Crawler]] will have three +1/+1 counters placed on it this way, making it a 3/6.

- However, what happens if we flip them?  Taking [[Hardened Scales]] into account first, 1 counter turns into 2.  Then taking [[Branching Evolution]] into account, 2 is doubled so it turns into 4.  [[Duskshell Crawler]] will have four +1/+1 counters placed on it this way, making it a 4/7.

**Order matters!**

- You're losing out on extra value if you multiply before adding.  **Always take addition effects into account first!**  This means [[Hardened Scales]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]] and [[Benevolent Hydra]].

- Then any multiplicative effects.  This means [[Branching Evolution]], [[Doubling Season]] and [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]].

Let's try another example, see if you can figure out how many +1/+1 counters should be placed for maximum value in the following scenario:

- You have [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]], [[Hardened Scales]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]], [[Ozolith, the Shattered Spire]] and [[Doubling Season]] in play

- You have 3 Experience Counters

- You move to combat and decide to put +1/+1 counters on the Kami using Ezuri's effect.  How many should it receive?

Answer: ||Twelve +1/+1 counters would be placed on the Hydra (3 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6, then doubled = 12)||

Starting to get the hang of it?  Let's get crazy with this last example:

- You have [[Ezuri, Claw of Progress]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]], [[Benevolent Hydra]], [[Hardened Scales]], [[Ozolith, the Shattered Spire]], [[Branching Evolution]], [[Doubling Season]] and [[Innkeeper's Talent]] with its final upgrade in play

- You have 5 Experience Counters

- You cast [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider|PKHM]] and move to combat.  You decide to put +1/+1 counters on Vorinclex using Ezuri's effect.  How many should he receive?

Answer: ||144 counters would be placed on Vorinclex (5 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 9, then doubled 4 times = 144)||

===center
![raider](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/ErKmXwBVEAE6at3?format=jpg&name=4096x4096)

*The strong are resilient, but only the strongest are feared*

===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Change Log

## Change Log

===accordion
===panel:12/15/24

- Decided I had enough ramp so ditching [[Wood Elves]] wasn't a big deal.  It was replaced with [[Shroofus Sproutsire]] because just read him - he's nuts in this deck.

- [[Guardian Project]] was replaced with [[Inspiring Call]] because it's a more aggressive form of card draw but also protection.

===endpanel
===panel:7/21/24

- [[Roaming Throne]] was removed for a few reasons.  There weren't enough elves to really make it worth it, and if Ezuri gets removed, it's doing nothing.  Also, I needed it for another deck and didn't want to buy another copy.  It was the easiest cut for [[Pollywog Prodigy]].

- [[Hardened Scales]] was removed for [[Innkeeper's Talent]].  It was the weakest enchantment in the deck and [[Innkeeper's Talent]] scales much better into the late game.

===endpanel
===panel:3/26/24

- [[Arcane Signet]] was replaced with [[Maraleaf Pixie]].  Same mana cost but Pixie gives us experience plus she's a flyer so she can become a big threat with +1/+1 counters.

- [[Chord of Calling]] was removed for [[Archdruid's Charm]] because it can also tutor a creature but also do several other things.

- [[Simic Ascendancy]] was removed because I was getting bored of it as a win-con.  [[Heroic Intervention]] was brought in for some extra board protection.

===endpanel
===panel:1/21/24

- Minor change - [[Hedge Maze]] is just objectively better than [[Tangled Islet]].

===endpanel
===panel:11/20/23

- Minor swap: [[Talisman of Curiosity]] for [[Ornithopter of Paradise]].  Costs 2, provides mana in the early game and can be a threatening attacker in the late game thanks to flying.

===endpanel
===panel:11/2/23

Some updates after the Lost Caverns of Ixalan spoilers:

- [[Ulvenwald Tracker]] was a bit too slow as far as removal goes, so it was replaced with [[Kitesail Larcenist]].

- [[Trygon Predator]] was also a bit slow, needing to wait to attack before it could destroy anything so [[Steelbane Hydra]] took its place.

- Had to make room for [[Roaming Throne]] and after a lot of thinking I decided on cutting [[Branching Evolution]] for now.

===endpanel
===panel:8/23/23

- I've found that [[Defiler of Vigor]] is seeing less and less utility ever since I loaded up on more instants and sorcery spells.  So it was the easiest cut to make once [[Elusive Otter]] was revealed.

- [[Scavenging Ooze]] was replaced by [[Agatha's Soul Cauldron]]; we still get the graveyard disruption but with a lot more utility.

- Ok, now for the big one.  This may seem blasphemous but I'm cutting [[Doubling Season]].  First off, I need it for another deck.  Second, it does nothing on its own.  5 mana for no immediate impact and it puts a huge target on my back.  This was the best cut I could find for the new card [[Court of Garenbrig]].

===endpanel
===panel:6/13/23

- [[Oko, Thief of Crowns]] was removed.  I often only got one activation out of him and the other three players would make a beeline to get rid of him.  Perhaps they're still traumatized by Eldraine Standard, lol.  Regardless, he's been replaced with [[Legolas Greenleaf]] and I'm happy with how he's performing.

===endpanel
===panel:4/28/23

- In an effort to add more interaction, [[An Offer You Can't Refuse]] and [[Swan Song]] were added.

- [[The Ozolith]] was removed.  This may have been a stupid decision but I was struggling to find a cut and after some honest evaluation I realized I didn't care too much if my creatures with counters died because the deck was so good at generating more.

- [[Master Biomancer]] was also removed because while he's a strong anthem effect, he pretty much disables the ability to generate exp counters with Ezuri so if he gets removed, we'll have to rebuild all the exp we missed out on.

- I have no idea if these changes were dumb but we'll see the next time I pull this deck out at game night.

===endpanel
===panel:4/4/23

- Found myself not really using [[Pact of Negation]] very much so it was the easiest cut to make room for the new [[Ozolith, the Shattered Spire]].

- Also replaced a single Forest with [[Turntimber Symbiosis]] because it can be played as an untapped green land so the opportunity cost is almost negligable AND gives us the option of a late-game mana sink.

===endpanel
===panel:3/30/23

- [[Farhaven Elf]] was removed in favor of [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]].  It's a 3-mana creature that ramps but is also a [[Hardened Scales]] which is incredible in this deck.

===endpanel
===panel:1/24/23

- Removed [[Ranger Class]] and replaced it with [[Master Biomancer]] for a couple of reasons.  [[Ranger Class]] requires 2 mana to play and another 2 to provide any sort of utility.  [[Master Biomancer]] also requires a total of 4 mana to play and provides +1/+1 counter buffing but with a much stronger static effect.  He was performing very well in the budget build and so I've decided to put him in here to see how he does.

===endpanel
===panel:1/11/23

- [[Awakening Zone]] was removed to make room for [[Heronblade Elite]].  The Zone was a bit too slow for my taste, doing nothing on the turn it came down and requiring a full rotation around the table to return any value, whereas [[Heronblade Elite]] immediately gives us an experience counter with Ezuri and can generate big mana just like [[Marwyn, the Nurturer]].

===endpanel
===panel:1/5/23

- Did even more testing and decided to remove [[Foundation Breaker]] in favor of [[Glen Elendra Archmage]] because having a repeatable [[Negate]] is fun, especially if it's on a creature body that can Persist repeatedly if we keep canceling out its -1/-1 counter with +1/+1 counters.

- To make up for the artifact/enchantment hate loss, I got my hands on [[Boseiju, Who Endures]] and [[Otawara, Soaring City]] and replaced a basic [[Forest]] and [[Island]] with them.

- Also had an honest evaluation of [[Alchemist's Refuge]] and acknowledged that in 3 years of playing this deck, I've used its Flash ability maybe one time.  This deck likes to play on curve as much as possible and any held mana goes toward instant-speed interaction, so [[Alchemist's Refuge]] might as well be a [[Wastes]].

- Made several updates to the Primer to reflect the recent changes.

===endpanel
===panel:1/4/23

- [[Mist-Cloaked Herald]], [[Gudul Lurker]] and [[Slither Blade]] were all removed.  Unblockable creatures are cool, but I wanted some more utility, so each one of these was replaced with another 1-mana creature with some form of evasion (Flying) and some form of utility which the aforementioned creatures were lacking.

- The replacements are [[Siren Stormtamer]], [[Mausoleum Wanderer]] and [[Hypnotic Siren]].  Each one is cheap to cast, has Flying and gives us an Exp Counter with Ezuri, so they can easily be loaded up with +1/+1 counters if we want to swing in with evasive damage but they have additional effects we can leverage as well.

===endpanel
===panel:12/27/22

- Took out [[Simic Signet]] because we have plenty of ramp already and replaced it with [[Ulvenwald Tracker]] because he's small enough to trigger Ezuri but also gives us a repeatable fight effect which is a great removal option for us.

===endpanel
===panel:12/1/22

- Replaced [[Pir, Imaginative Rascal]] with [[Benevolent Hydra]] because it provides (almost) the same static effect with the added utility of being able to redistribute its own counters which Pir can't do.

===endpanel
===panel:10/31/22

- Tested [[Ranger Class]] and liked it a lot, so much so that I replaced [[Swiftfoot Boots]].  The boots are nice but there's so much ramp in the deck that it's not terribly difficult to re-cast Ezuri if he gets removed.  [[Ranger Class]] creates a token that grants us an Exp counter, puts +1/+1 counters on our creatures and lets us cast creatures off the top of our deck.  It's a fantastic card for what we're trying to do.

===endpanel
===panel:10/5/22

- Very minor change: [[Rimewood Falls]] was replaced with [[Tangled Islet]] for aesthetic reasons.  A random snow land was bothering me and [[Tangled Islet]] is functionally the same otherwise.

===endpanel
===panel:9/25/22

- [[Noxious Revival]] was removed.  Its main purpose was retrieving [[Sage of Hours]] in case it was removed or countered earlier, but [[Finale of Devastation]] can do the same job.  I've added [[Mist-Cloaked Herald]] for now, it makes the deck slightly more aggressive and creature-focused.

===endpanel
===panel:9/1/22

- I wracked my head on what to cut for [[Defiler of Vigor]] and decided that the curve of the deck was too delicate, meaning I did not want to cut a lower CMC card in favor of the Defiler so it had to be a CMC 5 or higher card.  As much as I love [[Iridescent Hornbeetle]], it will be taking a back seat for a while as I test [[Defiler of Vigor]] in its place.

===endpanel
===panel:8/29/22

- Took some lessons learned from the budget build of this deck and decided to do a bit of an overhaul.

- All snow-covered basic lands were removed and replaced with regular basic lands of the same type.  [[Rimewood Falls]] will remain in the deck until [[Tangled Islet]] releases in Dominaria United.  I still like the idea of being able to find it with [[Wood Elves]], [[Nature's Lore]] and [[Three Visits]].

- [[Ice-Fang Coatl]] and [[Into the North]] were removed due to the removal of the snow lands.  The Coatl was fine but I wanted more unblockable threats because I was liking how [[Invisible Stalker]] was performing.  [[Into the North]] isn't necessary because we have so much ramp already and our curve isn't huge.

- Finally, [[Viral Drake]] was removed.  This was a tough decision but in gameplay I noticed that by the time I played [[Viral Drake]], my opponents were on the brink of death by regular combat damage anyway, so dealing Infect was a little overkill and the Proliferate activation isn't cheap.

- With those 3 cuts, I decided to add [[Bred for the Hunt]] because it was drawing me quite a few cards in the budget list.  And [[Slither Blade]] along with [[Gudul Lurker]].  1-drop unblockable creatures can become a menace very quickly in this deck and adding them justified the inclusion of [[Bred for the Hunt]].

===endpanel
===panel:7/24/22

- Decided to take out [[Toothy, Imaginary Friend]] in favor of [[Fathom Mage]].  It feels wrong running [[Pir, Imaginative Rascal]] without his Imaginary Friend but honestly [[Fathom Mage]] is just a more reliable card draw engine.  Toothy has to leave the field for us to benefit and that's just too slow for my taste.  I am also looking at replacing [[Guardian Project]] with either [[Bred for the Hunt]] or [[Primal Empathy]], will have to do some more testing to decide if it's worth it.  Finally, I'm looking to add [[Titanic Brawl]] but not sure what to cut yet.  Perhaps [[Pact of Negation]] but we'll see.

===endpanel
===panel:7/19/22

- Decided to take out [[Counterspell]] because I almost never used it.  I found myself tapping out to get power on board quite often, because this deck doesn't care if creatures get removed or killed.  There's enough ramp to recast Ezuri and keep playing.  I decided to put in [[Iridescent Hornbeetle]] because it was performing very well in the budget build.  I will also be testing [[Tanazir Quandrix]] as an additional finisher alongside [[Wild Beastmaster]] and [[Cultivator of Blades]].  Not sure what I'll cut for it yet, but looking at [[Toothy, Imaginary Friend]].

===endpanel
===endaccordion
